paragraphs.body: In the article, “What can neuroscience tell us about the mind of a serial killer?” John Parrington discusses how modern psychology, and neuroscience can bring light to what goes on inside the minds of serial killers. He supports his point by developing several important ideas. First, Parrington observes that serial killers have an absence of emotional awareness. For instance, a recent brain study was performed on past mass murders. He found that mass murders have a weakened connectivity between the amygdala and the prefrontal cortex. The amygdala processes the negative stimuli while the prefrontal cortex deciphers the responses from the amygdala. When the connectivity between these regions are weakened the processing of negative stimuli does not interpret into any conflicting emotions. This indicates why serial killers do not feel a sense of guilt, or sorrow about their measures. Next, he insinuates that those social influences play an important role in the evolution of serial killers. Most serial killers are insecure human beings that feel a need to murder due to an unhealthy fear of rejection. In particular, an individual who was abandoned or abused by a parent develops a feeling of unwantedness. By hurting the person they are yearning for, serial killers can eliminate the sense of rejection. Finally, Parrington points out that serial killers seem to decline in a sense of social goodness. We retain to distinguish right versus wrong from society, parents, and others yet serial killers perceive they are free from the penalty of these wrongs.

annotations.text: In the article, “What can neuroscience tell us about the mind of a serial killer?” John Parrington discusses how modern psychology, and neuroscience can bring light to what goes on inside the minds of serial killers.

paragraphs.body: You might be wondering why I just wrote about how psychology and neuroscience explains why serial killer commit murder. Well, there was on specific serial killer that caught my attention. Theodore Bundy, commonly referred as “Ted Bundy,” was a famous serial in the 1970’s that admitted to killing thirty women, but experts believe that it was over one hundred. Ted’s childhood was not such a “normal” childhood. His mother was secretly ashamed of her son and wanted to put him up for adoption. His mother’s parents did not want Ted to be put in the system, so they adopted him as their own. They told Ted that his mother was his sister, which had a great effect on him. Growing up Ted was part of the working-class family. He did well in school but not with his peers. At a young age he started showing interest in knives and killings, but no one saw that as odd at the time. Ted soon graduated from the University of Washington in 1972, where he got a degree in psychology. He then was accepted and attended law school in Utah. This package made him seem quite the catch to female colleagues. It was not until 1976 when he finally got caught and found guilty of his murders.

annotations.comment: This paragraph is actually another summary (of a documentary).  I wonder: do you need both of these summaries to "enter the conversation" of your essay?
annotations.context_left: emale colleagues. It was not until 1976 when he finally got caught and found guilty of his murders.

paragraphs.body: When I first watched a documentary about Ted Bundy, I was sitting right beside my mom with both of us in utter shock. His story was not like most serial killers. Instead of hiring a defense attorney to defend his case, Ted decided he was going to represent himself. His trials were broadcasted all over the United States where it caught many people attention. Many people thought Ted was innocent because he looked like a “normal” human being in society. But according to Parrington, “Ted Bundy, who was a “charming, handsome, successful individual [yet also] a sadist, necrophile, rapist, and murderer with zero remorse who took pride in his ability to successfully kill and evade capture.” Parrington’s point is that someone may look like a “normal” human being based on the qualities they showcase, but deep inside there is no such thing as normal.

paragraphs.body: So why does society categorize people as “being normal”? This question has always been present in my mind ever since I was a little girl. In society, people believe that in order to fit in or be considered “normal” they have to have specific qualities based on society norms. But these qualities should mean nothing to us. We are made to be unique in our way. So, why do people still want to change their appearances and personalities based on popular trends made by society? Why do we outcast people who look a little different from us? Why do we judge people by how they act? All these questions really made think about my own self.

annotations.text: So why does society categorize people as “being normal”? This question has always been present in my mind ever since I was a little girl.
annotations.comment: This is a fascinating question to arise from Ted Bundy's story.

annotations.text: why do people still want to change their appearances and personalities based on popular trends made by society? Why do we outcast people who look a little different from us? Why do we judge people by how they act? All these questions really made think about my own self.

paragraphs.body: As I got older, I have always felt the pressure to fit into society, or with my friends, because I feared rejection. I thought in order to fit into society I had to change who I was, but ultimately it was not worth it in the end. During my first two years of high school, I decided to start eating healthy and working out a lot more because it was the new trend on social media. As I grew more into this routine, the more it started to consume me. I saw myself changing from who I was. I was no longer this bubbly little girl, but someone who was so insecure about themselves that going out in public seemed like a chore. I was so focused on trying to fit into society that I lost myself along the way. But, with a lot of hard work and knowledge, it made me realize that I do not have to change myself because that is what society says I must do. I realize that my quirks make me different from every other person. I do not have to be considered “normal” in society’s eyes. My quirks shaped me into the person that I am today, and I like the person I became.

annotations.comment: So, in a way, you were different on the inside than you were on the outside?

paragraphs.body: Anytime you hear about Ted Bundy and his case, remember that he may have looked like a “normal” person on the outside based on his appearances, but in reality, there is no such thing as normal. Do not change yourself because that is what you think society will be considered normal. Stay true to yourself and let your quirks shine in public because changing into someone else is not worth it in the end.
